posts.name: Joseph Grassa
posts.cooked:
I just decided I am going to Matter of Taste in Zurich on April 2nd. I am really excited for the Biondi Santi Retrospective, but I have not yet purchased the Grand Walkabout Tasting tickets.

This is my first time going to an event like this, and I am completely oblivious on what to expect. Is it as obvious as it seems to me that I should at least purchase the Grand Walkabout tickets for the Saturday of the Biondi Santi Retrospective? Any other thoughts or advice is greatly appreciated, as again I am completely unaware of what to expect.

Link to the event: https://www.events.robertparker.com/

You should come to my Burgundy masterclass too!

posts.name: Jorg G
posts.cooked:
Good idea, William. Your burgundy class looks very interessting (the Bdx too). The wines: All the way up from south to north. Young ones though.
I think of just buying a ticket.

posts.name: Ethan Abraham
posts.cooked: Wow, I know Switzerland is supposed to be pricey but those seminars look like great deals. Hell the byo 15 course dinner, a seminar or two, plus a plane ticket to Geneva is less than the le paulee dinner.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Good idea, William. Your burgundy class looks very interessting (the Bdx too). The wines: All the way up from south to north. Young ones though.
I think of just buying a ticket.

Young ones, but we intentionally picked stuff that’s expressive. Arnoux-Lachaux 2018 Chambolle, for example, is very open.

I‘m in on Biondi and Will‘s class too

Did you 2 end up making it? If so what were your thoughts? I wish I could have gotten in on one of the other tastings with William. Walking the 2 big rooms was good, but a bit overwhelming.

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked:
I‘m in on Biondi and Will‘s class too

Did you 2 end up making it? If so what were your thoughts? I wish I could have gotten in on one of the other tastings with William. Walking the 2 big rooms was good, but a bit overwhelming.

Will‘s class was hyper interesting, learend a lot and also learned to that I can trust him, when it comes to his Burgundy scores. it‘s so hard to source Burgundies these days that you can‘t buy quantities and put a critic to test to see if you vibe. Tasting 8 wines, discoveries from less prestigious appelations which got relatively high scores from Will, I immediatly understood why Will scored these wines high and also saw that these were good calls.

Biondi Santi was interesting. I think these wines are overrated. Here are my thoughts: 8 Vintages of Biondi Santi Riserva 1975-2015 - CellarTracker

posts.name: Joseph Grassa
posts.cooked:
I‘m in on Biondi and Will‘s class too

Did you 2 end up making it? If so what were your thoughts? I wish I could have gotten in on one of the other tastings with William. Walking the 2 big rooms was good, but a bit overwhelming.

Will‘s class was hyper interesting, learend a lot and also learned to that I can trust him, when it comes to his Burgundy scores. it‘s so hard to source Burgundies these days that you can‘t buy quantities and put a critic to test to see if you vibe. Tasting 8 wines, discoveries from less prestigious appelations which got relatively high scores from Will, I immediatly understood why Will scored these wines high and also saw that these were good calls.

Biondi Santi was interesting. I think these wines are overrated. Here are my thoughts: 8 Vintages of Biondi Santi Riserva 1975-2015 - CellarTracker

Great to hear about Will. Sad I missed it.

Nice notes. Unfortunately, I think I agree about Biondi Santi, but I don’t know that I could make a full judgement without having a bottle over the course of many hours. Sangiovese changes so significantly over the course of hours with air and that process to me is really what makes any Brunello. One thing that seems to carry the price is the ability to age though. None of the wines seemed past it and almost all seemed too young still. Based on current enjoyment, the 1995 was actually my favorite and I found it to be the most complex, so we may have different preferences. The 1975 seemed to be the only that was a bit tertiary, which made it my #2 while the purity of the 1998 made it my #3. I do however think most of the younger wines will mostly show better than the 75 when their time comes, so if I had rated them that would not be the order.

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked:
Great to hear about Will. Sad I missed it.

Nice notes. Unfortunately, I think I agree about Biondi Santi, but I don’t know that I could make a full judgement without having a bottle over the course of many hours. Sangiovese changes so significantly over the course of hours with air and that process to me is really what makes any Brunello. One thing that seems to carry the price is the ability to age though. None of the wines seemed past it and almost all seemed too young still. Based on current enjoyment, the 1995 was actually my favorite and I found it to be the most complex, so we may have different preferences. The 1975 seemed to be the only that was a bit tertiary, which made it my #2 while the purity of the 1998 made it my #3. I do however think most of the younger wines will mostly show better than the 75 when their time comes, so if I had rated them that would not be the order.

That‘s a fair point. Usually it takes an evening to really unlock all the magic in great wines. On the other hand, a great, world class wine usually shows complex right from the go or at least one of eight vintages would. But maybe I‘m just not the right wine drinker for this type of austere wine (my top five wine properties are probably Rayas, Cheval Blanc, Margaux, Yquem, Chave… not really austere wines).

posts.cooked:

Sangiovese changes so significantly over the course of hours with air and that process to me is really what makes any Brunello. One thing that seems to carry the price is the ability to age though. On the other hand, a great, world class wine usually shows complex right from the go or at least one of eight vintages would. But maybe I‘m just not the right wine drinker for this type of austere wine (my top five wine properties are probably Rayas, Cheval Blanc, Margaux, Yquem, Chave… not really austere wines).

Makes sense. Sangiovese might not really be your thing. Next Sangiovese you open I suggest drinking it alone or with 1 other. Sample early, assess when to have the first glass, but then still drink it over 3-5 hours after that point.
